content.text: What Are Starter Scaffold Templates and Repo Structure

Starter scaffold templates are versioned, parameterized project generators that produce a fully structured repository when executed through the IDP scaffolding tool. For agent systems, a scaffold template generates not just a directory structure but a complete, opinionated starting point: src/agents/ with a base agent class, src/prompts/ with a system prompt template, src/tools/ with a tool registry pattern, evals/ with a golden dataset placeholder and eval harness configuration, tests/ with unit and integration test scaffolding, .github/workflows/ with all required CI gate configurations, and configuration files for the dependency policy (deps-policy.yaml), architecture tests, and Renovate bot. The developer starts coding business logic immediately rather than spending days on project setup.

The repository structure encoded in the template reflects the platform team's architectural decisions and quality standards. Every repository generated from the template has the same layout, making it immediately familiar to any engineer who has worked on another agent service at the organization. Cross-service code review, incident response across multiple services, and knowledge transfer between teams all benefit from consistent structure. The template enforces that certain files (CODEOWNERS, gate-config.yaml, deps-policy.yaml) always exist and always contain minimum required entries, preventing the structural gaps that cause compliance failures or CI issues later.
Why Scaffold Templates Matter

Project setup time is wasted work that does not produce business value. A senior engineer setting up a new agent service from scratch—creating the directory structure, wiring in CI workflows, configuring the dependency policy, setting up architecture tests, creating the eval harness skeleton—may spend two to five days. If the platform team has encoded all of that setup into a scaffold template, the same setup happens in 10 minutes through the IDP. The template encodes the result of the platform team's accumulated learning without requiring each new project to rediscover it.

Structural drift—where services start with the same template but diverge significantly over time—is a different problem that templates alone cannot solve. Scaffold templates solve the starting state; golden path documentation and architecture tests address ongoing structural compliance. Together they create a system where all agent repositories start from the same foundation and have automated checks that catch significant structural divergence over time.
Core Benefits
Reduces new service setup time from days to minutes by encoding platform expertise in executable templates.
Ensures all agent repositories start from a consistent, compliant structure with security and quality controls in place.
Eliminates 'forgot to add' failures where developers create repositories without CODEOWNERS, CI gates, or dependency policies.
Enables platform improvements to propagate to new services immediately via template updates.
Makes cross-service code review faster because reviewers can navigate any service's structure immediately from familiarity.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Template rigidity: highly opinionated templates may not accommodate unusual project requirements without a customization mechanism.
Template maintenance: templates must be updated as platform services evolve; stale templates generate repositories with outdated configurations.
Overgeneration: templates that include too much boilerplate create repositories that are larger than needed and require cleanup.
Divergence over time: templates solve the starting structure but not ongoing structural compliance; architecture tests are needed to catch post-scaffold divergence.
Language and framework lock: templates built for Python/FastAPI require separate templates for teams using Go or Node.js, increasing maintenance surface area.
How Scaffold Templates Work

A scaffold template is a directory of Jinja2 (or equivalent) template files combined with a scaffolding manifest that defines the parameters the template accepts. When the scaffolding tool (Backstage scaffolder, Cookiecutter, or a custom CLI) executes the template, it prompts the developer for parameter values (service name, team, model provider, initial tool count), applies those values to template expressions in every file and directory name, and renders the complete project structure to disk.

The rendered repository is immediately functional: it can be pushed to GitHub, CI workflows will trigger and pass (running on the placeholder golden dataset), and the developer can run the agent locally using the scaffolded quickstart script. The template also includes a post-generation hook that registers the new service in the IDP service catalog, creates the initial Dependabot configuration, and sends a notification to the team's Slack channel that a new service has been created.

Template versioning ensures that developers can see which version of the template their repository was generated from. A `.scaffold-version` file in the root records the template version, generation date, and generation parameters. When the template is updated, the platform team can run a migration script that identifies all repositories generated from an older version and creates PRs to apply the update—similar to how Renovate manages dependency upgrades, but applied to structural template changes.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Agent starter scaffold template generating a fully structured repository with pre-configured CI gates, dependency policy, eval harness, architecture tests, and runbook templates

content.text: When to Apply Scaffold Templates

Use scaffold templates for every new agent service or significant new agent capability (a new agent that will live in its own repository). For incremental additions to existing services—a new tool, a new prompt variant—use feature branch conventions and code review rather than scaffolding. The template's value is in creating the structural foundation; for incremental changes within an existing structure, the template overhead is not warranted.

Invest in template creation when you have three or more agent services that share a similar architecture. With fewer services, the template maintenance overhead may exceed the time saved. With more services, the consistency benefits and setup time savings accumulate to justify even significant template maintenance investment.
How to Implement

# backstage-template.yaml — Scaffolding template definition
apiVersion: scaffolder.backstage.io/v1beta3
kind: Template
metadata:
  name: agent-service-scaffold
  title: New Agent Service
  description: Scaffold a new production-ready agent service with all platform wiring
spec:
  parameters:
    - title: Service Details
      required: [serviceName, teamName, modelProvider]
      properties:
        serviceName:
          title: Service name (kebab-case)
          type: string
          pattern: "^[a-z][a-z0-9-]{2,30}-agent$"
        teamName:
          title: Owning team
          type: string
        modelProvider:
          type: string
          enum: [anthropic, openai, multi]
        primaryUseCase:
          title: Primary use case (for eval dataset seeding)
          type: string
          enum: [customer-support, document-analysis, code-generation, other]
  steps:
    - id: fetch-template
      action: fetch:template
      input:
        url: ./skeleton
        values:
          serviceName: ${{ parameters.serviceName }}
          teamName: ${{ parameters.teamName }}
          modelProvider: ${{ parameters.modelProvider }}
          primaryUseCase: ${{ parameters.primaryUseCase }}
    - id: create-repo
      action: publish:github
      input:
        repoUrl: "github.com?repo=${{ parameters.serviceName }}&owner=myorg"
        defaultBranch: main
        topics: ["agent-service", "${{ parameters.teamName }}"]
    - id: register-catalog
      action: catalog:register
      input:
        catalogInfoUrl: ${{ steps['create-repo'].output.remoteUrl }}/blob/main/catalog-info.yaml
    - id: notify-team
      action: notification:send
      input:
        to: "${{ parameters.teamName }}@myorg.slack.com"
        message: "New agent service ${{ parameters.serviceName }} created: ${{ steps['create-repo'].output.remoteUrl }}"
  output:
    links:
      - title: Repository
        url: ${{ steps['create-repo'].output.remoteUrl }}

# skeleton/ — Template directory structure
skeleton/
├── .github/
│   ├── workflows/
│   │   ├── ci.yml          # Eval gate + security gate + load gate
│   │   ├── release.yml     # Environment promotion workflow
│   │   └── drift-scan.yml  # Weekly architecture drift detection
│   ├── CODEOWNERS          # Pre-filled with team entries
│   └── pull_request_template.md
├── src/
│   ├── agents/
│   │   └── ${{values.serviceName}}_agent.py  # Base agent class
│   ├── prompts/
│   │   └── system_prompt.txt    # Templated system prompt
│   ├── tools/
│   │   └── tool_registry.py     # Empty tool registry
│   └── context/
│       └── context_builder.py   # Context construction pattern
├── evals/
│   ├── run_evals.py             # Eval harness entry point
│   ├── datasets/
│   │   └── golden_v1_placeholder.jsonl  # 10 seed examples
│   └── metrics/
│       └── baseline_accuracy.json  # Initial baseline (0.0)
├── tests/
│   ├── architecture/            # Architecture test skeletons
│   └── unit/                    # Unit test scaffolding
├── ops/
│   └── runbooks/
│       └── RUNBOOK_TEMPLATE.md  # Runbook template to fill in
├── docs/
│   └── architecture.md          # Architecture overview template
├── infrastructure/
│   └── terraform/               # Env provisioning template
├── deps-policy.yaml             # Approved LLM SDK list (inherits from platform)
├── gate-config.yaml             # CI gate thresholds
├── .renovate.json               # LLM SDK upgrade rules
├── catalog-info.yaml            # Backstage service catalog entry
└── pyproject.toml               # Pre-filled with approved dependencies
Worked Examples

Example 1 — New billing agent service: The billing team needs a new agent to handle invoice dispute queries. A developer opens the IDP scaffolding catalog, selects 'New Agent Service', fills in: service-name='billing-dispute-agent', team='billing', model='anthropic', use-case='customer-support'. In 8 minutes, a new repository is created with all CI workflows configured, CODEOWNERS entries for the billing team, 10 seed golden examples for customer support, and the architecture tests skeleton. The developer starts coding the first billing tool the same afternoon.

Example 2 — Template update propagated: The platform team updates the scaffold template to add a new 'output sanitization' module to the base agent class, following a security audit finding. The migration script identifies 12 repositories generated from the previous template version and creates PRs in each repository applying the base agent class change. Each PR is reviewed and merged within the team's standard sprint cycle. All 12 services adopt the security improvement without manual work from the individual teams.

Example 3 — Non-standard requirement accommodated: A research team wants to build an agent service using LangGraph rather than the standard agent base class. They use the scaffold template for the project structure, CI workflows, dependency policy, and eval harness, but override the src/agents/ directory with their custom LangGraph implementation. The template's hook mechanism allows them to skip the base agent class generation while retaining all other template outputs. This opt-out mechanism prevents the template from being abandoned entirely when one component does not fit.
Real-World Application

A technology company with 18 agent services and growing measured that new service setup was taking an average of 4.2 days before template introduction. After implementing a Backstage scaffold template, new service setup time dropped to 14 minutes. More significantly, the structural consistency metric (percentage of agent repositories with required files present: CODEOWNERS, gate-config.yaml, deps-policy.yaml, eval harness) improved from 67% to 100% for all services created after template introduction. Three security findings in older services that were missing CODEOWNERS and dependency policy were remediated proactively during the same initiative by running the migration script on pre-template repositories.
Common Mistakes
Over-generating boilerplate that developers immediately delete, causing frustration and reducing template adoption.
Not parameterizing enough: hard-coding team names or model providers in templates that should be configurable.
Forgetting to include the CI workflow files, which is the highest-impact component and the most frequently missed in manual setup.
Not versioning templates, making it impossible to identify which services were generated from which version and requiring manual migration tracking.
Treating the scaffold as complete after initial release without establishing a maintenance cadence for template updates.
Best Practices
Include a post-generation 'getting started' script that runs the test suite and starts the agent locally so developers can verify the scaffold works before writing a line of code.
Keep the scaffold lean: generate the minimum structure that is always needed and let developers add optional components themselves, or offer add-on modules as separate scaffold steps.
Test the scaffold template in CI: run it as part of the platform team's CI suite to catch broken templates before they reach developers.
Document what each generated file is and why it exists in a generated README so developers understand the scaffold's contents rather than treating it as magic.
Establish a quarterly template review where the platform team updates the template to reflect current platform standards and migrates existing repositories.
Performance and Scalability

Template rendering should complete in under 30 seconds and repository creation in under 2 minutes. The slowest step is typically the GitHub repository creation API call; optimize by using the GitHub API's repository template feature for the base structure and the scaffolding tool only for parameterization. The catalog registration step is a background job that does not block the developer.

For organizations maintaining multiple scaffold templates (Python agent, TypeScript agent, Go agent), create a template library with shared components that each language-specific template imports. CODEOWNERS format, CI workflow structure, and Renovate configuration are language-agnostic and can be shared. This reduces template maintenance to shared components that are updated once and propagate to all language-specific templates.
Tooling and Ecosystem

Catalog integration, parameterized templates, action plugins
Cookiecutter
CLI-based template rendering
Jinja2 templates, pre/post hooks, YAML configuration
Copier
Template rendering with update support
Diff-based template migration for existing repositories
GitHub Repository Templates
Base repo structure
Creates repos from a template with one click
degit
Lightweight template cloning
Shallow clone of template directory without git history

Security and Reliability Considerations

Scaffold templates that generate CI workflow files are a significant security surface. A compromised template repository that injects malicious code into CI workflows would affect every new service generated from it. Protect the template repository with branch protection requiring two reviewers from the security team for any CI workflow file changes. Sign template releases with GPG and verify signatures in the scaffolding tool before rendering. Monitor the template repository for unauthorized changes with automated file hash verification.

Templates that auto-create GitHub repositories using service account credentials are another risk surface. Use a dedicated scaffolding service account with the minimum required permissions (create repository, write to repository). Audit log all scaffold executions—who requested, what parameters were used, when it ran—to detect misuse. Rate-limit scaffolding requests to prevent automated abuse of the repository creation capability.
Key Takeaways
Scaffold templates reduce new agent service setup from days to minutes by encoding platform expertise in executable, parameterized generators.
Always include CI workflow files, CODEOWNERS, dependency policy, and eval harness skeleton as non-optional template outputs—these are the highest-value components.
Version templates and maintain migration scripts so existing services can adopt template improvements without manual re-implementation.
Test the scaffold template itself in CI to prevent broken templates from reaching developers.
Keep templates lean: generate the minimum required structure and allow opt-in customization rather than over-generating boilerplate.
